All reports
NEXQLOUD SEALED

Voice of Customer Report

The customer translator: what regulated buyers and AI vendors say in their own words about governing AI, and what to do with it.

Prepared by Fenn and Hart
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Regulated buyers and the AI vendors selling into them want one thing they cannot get today: proof that an AI did only what it was allowed to do, in a form an auditor or regulator can check without taking the vendor’s word for it. They already paste regulated data into ChatGPT, they already fear privilege waiver, and they already call SOC 2 a sales badge that fails to keep them safe. This report is built entirely from public sources, so its first recommendation is to commission a primary-interview pass that confirms these registers with named customers.

The 26 verbatim quotes in this report come from people who own the liability when AI touches regulated data: CISOs, Data Protection Officers, General Counsel, hospital IT reviewers, government-contractor compliance leads, and the founders trying to sell governance tools into all of them. Three wants run through their own words.

They want to keep protected data out of tools that are not covered by a contract. A self-described privacy expert states it flatly: “You cannot send your patient data to OpenAI ChatGPT. That would constitute a failure to protect your patient’s PHI.”1 A law-firm buyer extends the same logic to legal matter and privilege waiver.6

They want to stop their own people from leaking that data, because policy alone fails. “It is not uncommon for people to paste non public data into websites just to do json formatting,” one security leader writes, “So just telling people not to do something won’t accomplish much.”4

They want the thing that governs the AI to produce an exportable, audit-ready trail. A founder who built an LLM firewall describes the want directly: “Everything gets logged with full context. Exportable audit trail for compliance.”8

The biggest gap between how this category talks and how these buyers talk sits at the word “compliance.” Vendors sell compliance as a feature, a badge, or a discount. Buyers describe it as a gate they have to clear and a checkbox that does not actually keep them safe. A vendor founder puts the whole category’s posture into one line: “SOC2 is really only a sales enablement tool, nothing more.”12

The single highest-value language shift follows from that line. Stop leading with ‘audit trail’ and ‘governance’, which every rival now says in near-identical words, and lead instead with the customer’s own unmet test: a record a regulator can confirm without ever contacting the company. The buyers are already describing the artifact. The category has not started selling it.

1. Evidence Base and Recommended Primary Research

This report carries no first-party customer interviews. Every quote is sourced from public review and discussion platforms: 18 from Hacker News, 4 from TrustRadius, and 4 from PeerSpot. Reddit and Gartner Peer Insights were attempted and blocked by CAPTCHA, so they yielded no verbatim text. State that limit plainly to any reader weighing this report: these are practitioners writing in public about their own work, and none of them was interviewed about NexQloud Sealed.

That gap shapes the strongest move available now. Commission a primary-interview and case-study pass with AI-led custom interviews of named buyers across healthcare, financial services, and legal. Four things that the public quotes show but cannot pin down are exactly what such a pass would sharpen.

It would confirm the emotional registers under named attribution, putting a real name behind what is now a pseudonym. The anxiety in CUS-001 and the resignation in CUS-019 are vivid. A named CISO saying them on the record is harvestable as a testimonial and a reference.

It would test the buyer-versus-user split directly by interviewing the economic buyer and the end user inside the same regulated org, since the public record shows them from opposite sides but never in the same room.

It would map the hire timeline at named accounts: when the question first lands, what forces it from passive to active, and what the security review does to a deal.

It would settle the win and loss forces that the public quotes only gesture at, including whether forwardable proof beats a SOC 2 badge in a live procurement fight, which today rests on a single strong public quote (CUS-012).

2. What Customers Say in Their Own Words

2.1 “You cannot send your patient data to OpenAI ChatGPT”

The most consistent thing regulated buyers say is a hard prohibition, stated without hedging. They rule the tool out the moment regulated data is in play, before any upside enters the conversation. This appears across healthcare, legal, and government data alike, which marks it as a category-wide reflex that crosses every regulated industry.

“Privacy expert weighing in here. You cannot send your patient data to OpenAI ChatGPT. That would constitute a failure to protect your patient’s PHI. So no AI won’t be listening in your next health appointment. If it is its almost certainly a HIPAA violation.”1 Persona: Privacy/compliance lead. Stage: Awareness.
“Source: Ex Hospital IT. I wouldn’t risk it. It is too easy to write the wrong prompts and leak PHI… Unless sold as HIPAA compliant, and the conditions of use for that compliance are known… don’t trust it, for SAAS. This is stuff covered in your yearly HIPAA briefing folks.”2 Persona: Healthcare IT / security reviewer. Stage: Evaluation.

A security-minded practitioner draws the boundary at the trust line itself: the data may not leave a place the buyer controls, and commercial AI systems sit outside it.

“But until it fully runs locally, it shouldn’t used by anyone to upload their PHI into (unless you anonymize beforehand). And definitely shouldn’t allow use with commercial AI systems (ChatGPT, Claude, and certainly not Deepseek). I would actually be surprised if any of them are HIPAA-compliant.”3 Persona: Security-minded practitioner. Stage: Research.

The same prohibition governs legal matter, where the stakes shift from PHI to attorney-client privilege. A law-firm technology buyer explains why a firm cannot just open ChatGPT, and names the exact mechanism of the fear.

“The reason law firms can’t just use ChatGPT or Claude is that they can’t allow confidential or privileged client data (such as documents provided through the prompts) to be stored and hosted on a third party service like ChatGPT or Claude, as these companies may have to turn over client data in response to subpoenas… thus risking leakage of client data to third parties and potential privilege waiver. Services like Harvey and Legora solve this problem by accessing the LLMs through APIs, and all client data, prompts/responses, etc., are stored encrypted… For many law firms, this is 95% of what they’re paying for.”6 Persona: Legal / law-firm technology buyer. Stage: Evaluation.

That last clause names the size of the want. The boundary around the data is 95% of the purchase. The model’s intelligence is the other 5%.

2.2 “A $20-per-month subscription does not buy you privilege”

The legal buyers carry a sharper version of the prohibition: a fear that using the wrong tool destroys the legal protection that is their whole job to preserve. A General Counsel quoting a 2026 federal ruling makes the fear concrete and dated.

“Not confidential. This is the finding with the broadest implications. Anthropic’s policy expressly states that user prompts and outputs may be disclosed to ‘governmental regulatory authorities’ and used to train the AI model. Judge Rakoff found there was simply no reasonable expectation of confidentiality. As he put it, the tool ‘contains a provision that any information inputted is not confidential.’ … A $20-per-month subscription does not buy you privilege.”7 Persona: General Counsel / legal practitioner. Stage: Awareness.

2.3 “Most corporate employees just take the path of least resistance”

The second pattern reframes the problem from the buyer’s intent to the user’s behavior. Security leaders report that the leak happens through ordinary convenience, not malice, and that telling people to stop does nothing. This appears in the same breath as the prohibition above, which is the tension that defines the category: the people who set the rule cannot enforce it with a rule.

“In my experience most corporate employees just take the path of least resistance. It is not uncommon for people to paste non public data into websites just to do json formatting… So just telling people not to do something won’t accomplish much. Most corporate employees also somehow think they know better than the policy. Any company that doesn’t want to feed data into ChatGPT should need to proactively block both ChatGPT and any website serving as a wrapper over it.”4 Persona: Security/IT leader. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The scale of that path-of-least-resistance behavior shows up in numbers the practitioners cite to each other. Two quotes carry the figures.

“77 percent of these AI users have been copying and pasting data into their chatbot queries, the LayerX study says. A bit more than a fifth (22 percent) of these copy and paste operations include PII/PCI.”16 Persona: Security practitioner. Stage: Awareness.
“The median org now uses 28 distinct AI apps. Global 2000 companies average 129. ‘Shadow AI’ is an understatement. … 2.4% of orgs trigger DLP violations through AI tools. The #1 category is regulated data (57.6% of violations), followed by source code (23.8%).”18 Persona: Cloud-security vendor / data analyst. Stage: Awareness.

The buyers have a name for this and they use it without explanation. A June 2026 security-vendor job post treats it as a known job category.

“Big current focus is shadow AI, i.e. catching when employees paste sensitive data into ChatGPT, Claude, or whatever LLM showed up this week. You’d build the systems that detect it.”17 Persona: Security-vendor engineering. Stage: Awareness.

2.4 “None of that exists for AI agents yet”

The most distinctive language in the entire set names the missing layer in terms borrowed from a problem these engineers already solved. They describe AI agents as service accounts without the controls that service accounts have had for decades. The phrasing is precise, technical, and identical across independent posters, which marks it as the category’s emerging vocabulary.

“Claude Code gives you two options: approve every mkdir & npm test individually, or pass ‘–dangerously-skip-permissions’ & give the agent unrestricted access to your filesystem, network, and shell. Most devs end up on the second option within a week. We solved this for CI/CD and service accounts decades ago. Declarative policies, scoped permissions, audit trails. None of that exists for AI agents yet.”9 Persona: Security/authorization engineer. Stage: Research.
“At the same time a lot of my customer shared their concerns about coding agent like Claude code and the potential security risk for the enterprise. So I built Veto… An LLM firewall. A proxy that sits in front of any LLM API… Everything gets logged with full context. Exportable audit trail for compliance.”8 Persona: AI-tooling founder / security engineer. Stage: Research.

One architect names the shape of the answer the rest are circling, and names the limit of it honestly.

“A distributed agentic AI with provenance and audit trails won’t remove liability, but it’s a more defensible architecture than a single end-to-end chatbot for high-risk domains.”10 Persona: AI architect in regulated domains. Stage: Evaluation.

2.5 “The platform does not reveal its secrets easily”

The buyers who already run governance and privacy platforms describe a different pain: the tools they bought are slow and demand a dedicated specialist. These verbatims come from TrustRadius and PeerSpot reviews of OneTrust, BigID, and Microsoft Purview, so they describe the incumbents a regulated buyer compares against. The complaint about configuration burden and disconnected tooling appears across all three products alike.

“The platform does not reveal its secrets easily. You will be rewarded if you can allocate a team member to specialize on the platform.”19 Persona: GRC / privacy program manager. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Some modules need to be customized in order to function correctly, rather than being useful out of the box… The modules lack pre-configured workflows, so there can be a lot of time spent on customized configuration.”20 Persona: Legal / compliance director. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“In my experience, Purview hasn’t come far enough yet to help us reduce the number of solutions that interact with each other.”23 Persona: Data governance / security practitioner. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The detection of regulated data itself draws complaints, which matters for a product whose entire promise rests on knowing what the AI touched.

“One area for improvement is the detection of data types. This is really important.”25 Persona: Data governance practitioner. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“There are some limitations to the solution with regard to the lineage of data from different systems.”26 Persona: Data governance / compliance lead. Stage: Use & Succeed.

A Data Protection Officer running OneTrust names the same gap from the discovery side, where finding the regulated data is the whole point.

“Data Discovery speed, today is slow and does not integrate all databases.”21 Persona: Data Protection Officer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

3. The Emotional Undercurrent

The dominant emotion is anxiety, and it names a single fear: being the person who let regulated data leave the boundary. CUS-001 carries it as dread of a HIPAA violation. CUS-007 carries it as dread of waiving privilege with a twenty-dollar subscription. The privacy expert’s “almost certainly a HIPAA violation”1 is the voice of someone protecting themselves before they protect a product.

Resignation runs underneath the governance-platform reviews. The OneTrust user who writes that “The platform does not reveal its secrets easily”19 has accepted that the tool requires a dedicated specialist and is advising the next buyer to staff for it. That flat, worn tone is the sound of a buyer who already paid for governance and got homework.

Vindication shows up in the engineers who name the missing layer. The OpenFGA maintainer’s “We solved this for CI/CD and service accounts decades ago… None of that exists for AI agents yet”9 carries quiet certainty about the gap. They watched the industry solve the same problem once with scoped permissions and audit trails, and they are stating that nobody has solved it again for AI agents.

3.1 The buyer and the user want opposite things

The clearest finding in this section is a split inside the same organization. The economic buyer and the end user carry opposite emotional registers, and that split is the reason a single message will not reach both.

The end user feels friction and routes around it. The security leader’s account of employees who “paste non public data into websites just to do json formatting” and who “somehow think they know better than the policy”4 is a portrait of a user who wants the rule to disappear. The user’s want is speed.

The buyer feels exposure and wants a wall. The same leader’s prescription, that a company “should need to proactively block both ChatGPT and any website serving as a wrapper over it,”4 is the buyer’s want: a hard boundary the user cannot cross. The buyer’s want is control.

The vendor founders sit on a third seat and feel a different pressure: the deal-killing security review. Their register is neither friction nor exposure. It is the dread of momentum dying at handoff, which Section 4 takes up directly. Messaging that speaks to the buyer’s need for control will read as friction to the user, and copy that promises the user speed will read as recklessness to the buyer. Segment the message to the seat, not the company.

4. Why They Hired

The hire follows a recognizable arc, and the public quotes line up along it from first thought to habit. Read in order, they show how a regulated buyer moves from a vague worry to a governed boundary.

  1. First thought (Awareness). The question lands as a flat prohibition someone says out loud. “You cannot send your patient data to OpenAI ChatGPT,”1 and the legal version, “A $20-per-month subscription does not buy you privilege.”7 The pull at this stage is fear of a specific, nameable failure.
  2. Passive looking (Research). The buyer starts reading and finds others naming the missing layer. “We solved this for CI/CD and service accounts decades ago. Declarative policies, scoped permissions, audit trails. None of that exists for AI agents yet.”9 The pull is recognition that a real category of control is absent.
  3. Active evaluation (Evaluation). The buyer tests architectures against the prohibition. A law-firm buyer explains that the API-and-encryption pattern is “95% of what they’re paying for,”6 and an architect frames the standard a tool has to meet: “a distributed agentic AI with provenance and audit trails… a more defensible architecture than a single end-to-end chatbot for high-risk domains.”10 The pull is a defensible boundary they can stand behind.
  • Decision and first use (Purchase, Use and Succeed). The product earns its keep when it answers the security review and produces the trail. The founder who built the LLM firewall states the deliverable customers asked him for: “Everything gets logged with full context. Exportable audit trail for compliance.”8

Habit. The tool becomes the place the boundary lives. The governance comfort a buyer wants to reach is named by an access-governance founder who watched it happen with other tools.

“Vendor security questionnaires become easy to answer when we, as an organisation, use our product. We have seen reproducibility and access auditability in organisations that adopt products that access schema and metadata from databases, compute infrastructures, etc. comforts customers. Your customers care about security incidents like unauthorised access, privilege abuse, accidental operations, insider threats, etc. on the vendor’s side which in my opinion are real threats.”15 Persona: Enterprise-AI / access-governance founder. Stage: Evaluation.

One buyer states the job the whole arc serves, in the plainest possible terms.

“Add context to data to get a bigger picture and make decisions regarding compliance, privacy, coverage, security, and governance.”22 Persona: Healthcare IT analyst. Stage: Use & Succeed.

5. Why They Almost Didn’t

The hesitations sort into the four forces that pull a buyer toward or away from a switch. The push and pull get them moving. The anxiety and habit hold them back. Each force here is in the customer’s own words.

The push (the pain of staying put). The vendor founders feel the deal stalling and say so. One names the cost of waiting on compliance.

“We’re an early B2B SaaS company that just raised a small pre-seed round. Like many others, we’ve been telling ourselves we’ll worry about SOC 2 ‘once a big customer asks.’ But now I’m wondering if that mindset is quietly keeping us out of conversations and deals we don’t even realize we’re missing.”11 Persona: AI-vendor / SaaS founder selling into regulated buyers. Stage: Awareness.

The pull (the promise of the new way). The pull is a boundary that proves itself, named by the firewall founder (“Exportable audit trail for compliance”8) and the access-governance founder who watched auditability “comfort” customers.15

The anxiety (fear of the new tool). The deepest hesitation is that the security review will expose every gap. Sellers describe the exact moment a deal turns from value to interrogation.

“When you pitch your product to your business sponsor… once they start to involve IT and procurement and security, then all of a sudden the focus shifts away from value and towards risk and cost management… otherwise, as you note, they’ll throw big Excel sheets at you with questions for which many of your answers will likely be ‘No’.”13 Persona: Enterprise-software seller. Stage: Purchase.

The questionnaire itself draws a near-comic frustration that is really a plea for a tool that answers it cleanly.

“I was just lamenting the state of these forms today. I feel like many of the questions are variations on the theme of ‘what antivirus software do you use to protect your basement mainframe from your corporate network traffic?’ ‘N/A’ isn’t a long enough answer, so it feels like you need to keep explaining ‘the Internet’… Is there a modern, no-nonsense guide to filling these out honestly without telling the person doing the checkbox checking that their form is dumb?”14 Persona: Vendor engineer answering security reviews. Stage: Purchase.

A government-contractor compliance lead adds the regulated-buyer side of the same review: the vetting that any vendor has to survive.

“Well, GP was referring to blocking ChatGPT as a federal contractor. I suspect that as a federal contractor, they are also vetting other people that they share data with, not just blocking ChatGPT as a one-off thing… generic federal data isn’t as tightly regulated as, say, HIPAA PHI (having spent quite a lot of time working for a place that handles both), but there are externally-imposed rules and consequences, unlike simple internal-proprietary data.”5 Persona: Government-contractor compliance lead. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The habit (the comfort of the current way). The strongest brake is the buyer who already owns a heavy platform and dreads adding another. The OneTrust and Purview reviewers carry it: the tool that “does not reveal its secrets easily”19 and the “modules [that] lack pre-configured workflows”20 taught these buyers that governance tools cost a specialist. A Purview admin adds the purchase-stage version of the same fatigue.

“Licensing is frustrating due to multiple modules and licenses. The feature might be available in the console, but it won’t work without the proper license, creating confusion.”24 Persona: IT / governance administrator. Stage: Purchase.

6. The Language Gap

Customers and vendors describe the same problem in different words. The vendor column below holds the category cliche that any number of competitors use. The shift column is the customer’s own language promoted into the message.

What customers say (verbatim) What vendors say (the category cliche) The shift to make
“You cannot send your patient data to OpenAI ChatGPT”1 ‘AI-powered productivity’ / ‘enterprise-ready AI’ Lead with the prohibition the buyer already enforces, then show how the boundary holds
“A $20-per-month subscription does not buy you privilege”7 ‘secure and compliant’ Name the legal protection at stake (privilege, PHI), beyond generic ‘security’
“None of that exists for AI agents yet”9 ‘AI guardrails’ / ‘trust layer’ Say ‘scoped permissions and an audit trail for AI agents’, the service-account language they use
“Exportable audit trail for compliance”8 ‘full visibility’ / ‘real-time monitoring’ Promise an exportable record, beyond ‘visibility’
“SOC2 is really only a sales enablement tool, nothing more”12 ‘SOC 2 certified’ as the headline trust signal Treat the badge as table stakes; lead with proof a regulator can check
“The platform does not reveal its secrets easily”19 ‘intuitive’ / ‘easy to deploy’ Promise it works without a dedicated specialist, and show it

Words to adopt: PHI, privilege, privilege waiver, shadow AI, scoped permissions, audit trail, exportable, provenance, ‘path of least resistance’, ‘without ever contacting the company’, ‘necessary but not sufficient’.

Words to drop: AI-powered, enterprise-ready, trust layer, real-time visibility, intuitive, seamless, the standalone ‘SOC 2 certified’ headline used as the main proof.

7. What Customers Feel but Do Not Say

These are patterns the quotes support without stating outright. They are inferred from what the buyers circle around, never imposed.

They want a way to check the vendor’s claims that does not depend on the vendor. The access-governance founder gets close when describing how customers care about “unauthorised access, privilege abuse, accidental operations, insider threats… on the vendor’s side.”15 The unspoken want underneath is independent verification, a record they can confirm themselves instead of reading a report and hoping.

They suspect the certification they ask for does not protect them. The vendor founder says the quiet part directly with “SOC2 is really only a sales enablement tool,”12 and the questionnaire frustration in CUS-014 carries the same buried doubt: the forms feel like theater that does not measure real security. Buyers keep asking for the badge while half-believing it proves nothing.

They are tired, and they expect a governance tool to add work. Nobody writes “I am exhausted.” They write that the platform “does not reveal its secrets easily,”19 that modules need heavy configuration,20 and that licensing “creates confusion.”24 The unvoiced complaint is that governance has always meant more burden, so they brace for it.

8. Rave Notes

A testimonial and proof-language bank a copywriter can pull from directly, grouped by theme. Every line is a real customer or practitioner voice with a citation. Few of these are conventional praise, because this category’s strongest advocacy is a sharp statement of the problem the product solves.

On the hard prohibition (use to open with the buyer’s own rule):

“You cannot send your patient data to OpenAI ChatGPT. That would constitute a failure to protect your patient’s PHI.”1
“The reason law firms can’t just use ChatGPT or Claude is that they can’t allow confidential or privileged client data… thus risking leakage of client data to third parties and potential privilege waiver… For many law firms, this is 95% of what they’re paying for.”6
“A $20-per-month subscription does not buy you privilege.”7

On the missing governance layer (use to name the category):

“We solved this for CI/CD and service accounts decades ago. Declarative policies, scoped permissions, audit trails. None of that exists for AI agents yet.”9
“A distributed agentic AI with provenance and audit trails won’t remove liability, but it’s a more defensible architecture than a single end-to-end chatbot for high-risk domains.”10
“Everything gets logged with full context. Exportable audit trail for compliance.”8

On the failure of policy and the scale of shadow AI (use to establish urgency):

“So just telling people not to do something won’t accomplish much… Any company that doesn’t want to feed data into ChatGPT should need to proactively block both ChatGPT and any website serving as a wrapper over it.”4
“The median org now uses 28 distinct AI apps. Global 2000 companies average 129. ‘Shadow AI’ is an understatement.”18

On the checkbox versus real proof (use to reframe SOC 2):

“SOC2 is really only a sales enablement tool, nothing more. So it’s really how many enterprise deals you will lose without SOC2 vs. how many you will win.”12
“Vendor security questionnaires become easy to answer when we, as an organisation, use our product.”15

9. Strategic Implications

This report exists to inform how the Marketing and Messaging Guide builds perception around the source of truth, which rests on the leadership interviews. It surfaces where customer language diverges from that source so the messaging can close the gap. It does not argue that NexQloud Sealed should become whatever public reviewers want. Each implication below ends in an operative move tied to a Brief recommendation or an MMG pillar.

Commission the primary-interview and case-study pass first. Every register, split, and force in this report comes from public posts by people who were never asked about Sealed. Before the messaging hardens, commission an AI-led primary-interview and case-study program across the healthcare, financial-services, and legal beachhead, and use it to confirm the emotional registers, the buyer-versus-user split, the hire timeline, and the win/loss forces named above. Commission it.

  • Lead with the proof artifact, not the governance language. The buyers already describe the want, from “Exportable audit trail for compliance”8 to the architect’s “provenance and audit trails.”10 The category now says ‘governance’ and ‘audit trail’ in near-identical words, so those phrases no longer separate Sealed from a rival. Reposition the lead onto the one claim no competitor matches, the record a regulator can confirm without ever contacting the company, and anchor the brand there (reinforces the Brief’s reposition-to-lead recommendation, ARG-1).

Demote the SOC 2 badge to table stakes. Customers warn in their own words that the certification is “only a sales enablement tool, nothing more.”12 Leading on the badge sells a commodity buyers half-distrust. Keep SOC 2 as a baseline the buyer expects, lead with forwardable proof that survives the security review, and demote the badge from headline to footnote (reinforces the dual-ICP wedge, ARG-6).

  • Speak to the seat, not the company. The buyer wants a wall and the user wants speed, and the quotes show them wanting opposite things inside one org.4 Write one message for the liability owner that promises control and a checkable record, and a second for the end user that promises the governed path is faster than the workaround, and segment the message by seat (reinforces the dual-ICP MMG pillar).

Aim the message at the security-review moment. Sellers describe the deal dying when “the focus shifts away from value and towards risk and cost management.”13 Build the core message and sales assets around the moment IT, procurement, and security enter the deal, and position Sealed as the thing that answers the Excel sheet of questions cleanly, then arm the champion with a forwardable proof packet (reinforces the dual-ICP wedge, ARG-6).

Reviews are the new sales floor. The buyers in this report are reading TrustRadius, PeerSpot, and Hacker News mid-evaluation and forming their shortlist from what they find there, and the incumbents’ weaknesses (the platform that “does not reveal its secrets easily,”19 the “modules [that] lack pre-configured workflows”20) are public and quotable. The review surface is a live sales channel, so seed and manage the public review presence with the same discipline applied to the website, and surface the Sealed governance story where a buyer running diligence actually looks (reinforces the surface-the-proof wedge, ARG-2).

Sources

  • Hacker News, comment on ‘AI might be listening during your next health appointment’ (Axios), poster more_corn (self-described privacy expert), 2023-09-26, https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=37664647

  • Hacker News, comment on ‘Two kinds of AI users are emerging’, poster Antibabelic quoting The Register, 2026-02-02, https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46853214
  • Hacker News, ‘Ask HN: Who is hiring? (June 2026)’, Obsidian Security job post by volker48, 2026-06-01, https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48361574
  • Hacker News, Show HN comment by emeryray02 (Netskope team) presenting AI-app telemetry dashboard, 2026-03-12, https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47353138
